nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral and rocky reefs, found solitary or in small loose groups on mixed coral and rubble areas of lagoons and outer slopes at depths of roughly 2–31 m.

decorPreferences: fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep for burrowing

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates in the wild, including small crustaceans, flatworms, and pyramidellid snails. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and quality marine pellets or flakes. Tends to do best with several small feedings per day.

summary: A small, brightly coloured Western Indian Ocean wrasse with iridescent tangerine stripes over a bluish-green body. Generally hardy and considered reef-safe with caution, though it tends to predate on small ornamental invertebrates.
careNotes: Best housed in a well-established saltwater aquarium with abundant live rock for foraging and refuge, open swimming space, and a fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep — the species buries itself in the sand at night and when startled, so a coarse or thin substrate is generally considered unsuitable. A tightly fitting lid is widely recommended, as Halichoeres wrasses have been known to jump from open-top tanks. Generally peaceful toward unrelated fish, but tends to be territorial toward other Halichoeres or similarly shaped wrasses; best kept singly, as a male/female pair, or as a small harem of one male with several females. Considered reef-safe with caution — it does not directly harm corals or clams and has been reported to control flatworms and pyramidellid snails, but cannot distinguish between desirable and undesirable invertebrates and will tend to eat ornamental shrimp, fan worms and small crustaceans. Like most Halichoeres species, it is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite. Tank-size recommendations across sources varied widely (LiveAquaria 50 US gal / ~190 L vs ReefApp 400 L); given its active swimming habit, open-space requirement and territoriality toward conspecifics, the larger 400 L value was chosen. Reported temperature ranges also varied (LiveAquaria 22–26°C; FishBase wild data 25–29°C; ReefApp ~26°C) — a blended 24–27°C range was used.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been meaningfully documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquaria. In the wild the species pairs during spawning and is a protogynous hermaphrodite.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care level, temperament, reef compatibility, water parameters (temp, pH, SG, dKH), max size, family, origin, and 50 US gal minimum tank size.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), native range (Western Indian Ocean to Natal, South Africa; Socotra), max size (13 cm TL), depth range (2–31 m), preferred temperature (25.1–29.3°C), and reef-associated habitat. IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13 cm), sand-burying behaviour, jumping risk, protogynous hermaphroditism, harem grouping (one male with several females), and a 400 L minimum tank recommendation. Larger tank value adopted per playbook tank-size rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has not been meaningfully achieved in home aquaria. Protogynous hermaphrodite — females may transition to male under social pressure.
